sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Semi-Markov Conditional Random Fields (semi-CRFs) assign labels to segments of a sequence rather than to individual positions, enabling exact inference over segment-level features and principled uncertainty estimates at their boundaries. However, existing implementations must materialize a large edge potential tensor whose size grows with sequence length, maximum segment length, and label count, becoming prohibitive for speech-scale state spaces and intractable at genomic scales where sequences can exceed 100,000 positions. This memory bottleneck has limited the adoption of exact segment-level inference for long sequences and large label sets. We identify that the core inefficiency is materializing edge potentials that can instead be evaluated on-the-fly from a compact prefix-sum array, and make several improvements. First, replacing the stored edge tensor with prefix-sum lookup reduces the memory footprint by a factor proportional to the product of segment length and label count. Second, a streaming forward-backward pass with checkpoint-boundary normalization keeps working memory sublinear in sequence length while preserving exact gradients. Third, zero-centered cumulative scores control numerical drift and induce an adaptive duration prior under label imbalance. We integrate these ideas into Flash-SemiCRF, a fused Triton kernel that enables exact semi-CRF inference on previously intractable problem sizes. Available at https://github.com/biobenkj/flash-semicrf.

sections.title: 1 Introduction
sections.content: Many sequence types are naturally segmented and prediction tasks need to go beyond per-position labels. Modern neural encoders learn rich cross-position dependencies, but their output layers remain perposition: the loss decomposes over individual positions, no constraint enforces that adjacent predictions form valid segmentation, segment duration is never explicitly modeled, and there is no direct mechanism to obtain posterior marginals over segment boundaries or segment identities. Semi-Markov conditional random fields (semi-CRFs) [1] formalizes discriminative segment-level prediction by scoring variable-length segments via content, duration, and transition potentials. This factorization provides three structural properties that per-position classifiers lack, such as: every position belongs to exactly one labeled segment, explicit duration modeling that captures label-dependent length distributions, and segment-level posterior marginals that quantify uncertainty over boundaries rather than individual positions. The integration of CRFs as neural network output layers [25] demonstrated that structured inference and representation learning are complementary, with the CRF layer enforcing global coherence over the encoders learned representations. Rush [6] provided general-purpose semi-CRF implementation (pytorch-struct), making the algorithm widely accessible as training and inference layer but remaining bound by the edge tensor at scale. Semi-CRF inference requires materializing dense edge tensor of size O(T KC 2) (T positions, maximum segment duration, labels), dominating both time and memory. For NLP tasks with in the tens to hundreds and small K, this cost is manageable. At genomic scales, modern CRF-based tools have primarily adopted the per-position linear (K=1) case that avoids this edge tensor and the associated time and memory costs of standard semi-CRF: Bonito [7] and Dorado [8] decode raw nanopore signal, and Tranquillyzer [9] annotates long-read RNA-seq, all at sequence lengths routinely exceeding 105. Earlier semi-CRF applications to gene prediction CONRAD [10] and CRAIG [11] relied on hand-crafted features and CPU-bound Viterbi decode rather than serving as differentiable layers under neural encoder training. Extending to the semi-Markov setting multiplies the per-position cost by K; at genomic scales (K 103), materializing the full edge tensor becomes infeasible on GPU long before the sequential sweep over becomes the bottleneck. Recent work from Zaratiana et al. [12] addresses this problem through pruning. Their Filtered SemiCRF uses local span classifier to discard irrelevant segments before running structured inference on the reduced graph. This exploits the sparsity of named entities in text, where most spans are non-entities. In contrast, for dense segmentation problems where every position belongs to labeled segment such as those in genomics, the sparsity assumption does not typically hold and the full semi-CRF graph must be traversed. FlashAttention [13] demonstrated that for attention, the dominant cost is not arithmetic but memory access: by never materializing the attention matrix and instead recomputing blocks on-chip, it achieves large wall-clock speedups despite higher FLOP counts. We apply the same IO-aware principle to semi-CRF inference. The standard implementation pre-computes and stores the full O(T KC 2) edge tensor in HBM, then runs forward-backward over the stored result. At scale, this tensor dominates memory. We observe that when segment content scores decompose as sums over positions [1], prefix sums reduce per-edge evaluation to O(1), and the edge tensor need never be materialized. Each potential can instead be computed on-the-fly within streaming dynamic programming (DP) kernel from an O(T C) cumulative score array. We present Flash-SemiCRF, Triton-accelerated semi-CRF library built on this observation. The key ideas are: 1. On-the-fly edge computation. prefix-sum decomposition of segment content scores enables O(1)- in-duration edge potential evaluation, reducing inference from memory-bound to compute-bound and dispensing with the O(T KC 2) edge tensor entirely. 2. Ring-buffer streaming. The forward-backward algorithm operates through K-slot and 2K-slot ring buffers for the forward and backward passes respectively, achieving O(KC) working memory independent of sequence length . 3. Sublinear checkpointing. Gradient checkpointing at intervals, following the recomputation paradigm of FlashAttention [13] and the chunked scan of Mamba [14], yields O((cid:112)T /K KC) checkpoint memory that grows sublinearly in . 4. Globally-centered emissions. sequence-level baseline subtraction simultaneously stabilizes prefix sums at large and induces an adaptive, data-dependent duration prior that regularizes segmentation under label imbalance. 2 We validate correctness through training convergence criteria, finite-difference gradient checks, and self-consistency between independent forward-backward implementations. We benchmark against pytorch-struct [6] and demonstrate that sparse or banded matrix representations are incompatible with semi-CRF inference due to the triangular, rather than diagonal, sparsity structure of the intermediate operators. We characterize the transition from memory-bound to compute-bound scaling as function of , K, and C. Flash-SemiCRF provides structured decoding approach that can be applied to learned representations to produce segmentations that respect duration constraints and maintain segment-level coherence, properties that per-position classifiers typically discard.

sections.title: 2 Terminological clarification
sections.content: A linear CRF (or linear-chain CRF) is model in which every segment has duration one (K=1), so inference reduces to the standard per-position Viterbi/forwardbackward recurrence. linear scan is an algorithm that implements the semi-CRF dynamic program by sweeping left-to-right over positions, as opposed to tree-structured (parallel-scan) alternatives. The two are orthogonal: linear scan can execute semi-CRF inference at both K=1 and 1, and linear CRF can in principle be computed by any backend. Throughout this paper, linear CRF refers to the K=1 model, while linear scan refers to the sequential sweep algorithm.

sections.title: 3.1 Edge Potential Decomposition
sections.content: The prefix-sum decomposition of segment content scores [1] enables O(1)-per-edge evaluation from an O(T C) cumulative score array, reducing the memory footprint by factor of KC relative to the pre-materialized O(T KC 2) edge tensor. Existing implementations use this decomposition to efficiently pre-compute the full edge tensor. We instead evaluate each potential on-the-fly within the streaming DP kernel, so the tensor is never materialized. The following subsections define the cumulative score array, the edge potential it supports, and boundary projections that extends the standard three-term decomposition.

At genomic scale (T > 105), segment scores St+k,c St,c risk catastrophic cancellation, when both operands reach magnitude O(T ) while their difference is O(1). Subtracting the per-label mean before accumulation produces zero-mean random walk whose cumulative sums have standard deviation O( ), reducing the dynamic range by factor of .

The O(T C) cumulative score array replaces the O(T KC 2) pre-materialized edge tensor, reducing the memory footprint by factor of KC.

sections.title: 3.4 Boundary Projections
sections.content: The content score St,c Stk,c aggregates encoder emissions uniformly over all positions in segment, but carries no information about the positions at which segments begin or end. The transition matrix Tc,c captures label-to-label preferences but is position-independent. For applications where segment boundaries have distinctive local signatures, such as exon-intron transitions in genomic annotation, beat onsets in physiological signals, etc., explicit boundary features can improve segmentation accuracy. The optional boundary projections score segment start and end positions directly from the encoders ht and analogously for end, with hidden representation via independent linear heads, start ht Rd the encoder hidden state at position and start, end RCd learned projection matrices. segment [s, + k) with label receives additive boundary contributions start t,c = start s,c + end s+k1,c. Separate start and end projections reflect structural property of many segmentation problems: segment entry and exit involve categorically different signals (e.g., the start of person identity in language has different best indicators, the first name, than the end indicators, the last name). The separable form also has computational advantage: each endpoint requires only single lookup into precomputed (B, T, C) array, whereas joint parameterization ϕ(c, hs, hs+k1) would require O(T KC) precomputation over all valid endpoint pairs. The projections operate on the full encoder hidden states ht rather than the label-space scores fθ(t, c), allowing boundary features to capture context that may not be linearly decodable in label space. When disabled (P start = end = 0), Equation (4) reduces to the standard three-term decomposition. Relationship to classical semi-CRF features. In the classical formulation [1], segment features have the general form k(yj, yj1, x, tj, uj), where each feature may depend jointly on the segment label, the preceding label, the input, and both endpoint positions, at the cost of materializing the full O(T KC 2) edge tensor. Our boundary projections are the streaming-compatible factorization of this capability: boundary scores depend on position and destination label c, but not on segment duration or source label c, restricting boundary features to the separable form ϕ(yj, x, tj) + ϕ(yj, x, uj). This is the price of the O(KC) memory guarantee, start and end are precomputed before the DP loop and cannot condition on quantities only known at recurrence time.

sections.title: 4 Emission Baseline Centering
sections.content: The emission baseline subtraction (Equation 1) serves dual purpose. Numerically, it controls cumulative sum magnitude as described in Section 3.2. But per-label centering is not path-invariant, and it simultaneously induces an adaptive duration prior with useful regularization properties. We analyze this modeling effect here so that the reader enters the algorithm with complete understanding of what the edge potentials encode.

sections.title: 4.1 The Adaptive Duration Prior
sections.content: A path-invariant centering, such as subtracting per-position scalar bt = maxc fθ(t, c) shared across labels, would cancel in the partition function because every valid segmentation covers each position exactly once. Per-label centering with νb,c does not cancel, because different segmentations assign different labels to different positions and νb,c varies across labels. 4 The effective segment score under centering (Equation 3) is: Seff(c, s, k) = s+k1 (cid:88) u=s fθ(u, c) + Bk,c νb,c (5) The term νb,c acts as data-dependent duration prior : labels with high average emission νb,c incur larger penalty for long segments, while labels with low average emission receive relative boost. This is equivalent to canonical semi-CRF with an effective duration model Beff k,c = Bk,c νb,c that adapts per-sequence based on the global emission statistics. This decomposition has an empirical Bayes-like feature [15]: Bk,c captures population-level duration priors across training sequences, while νb,c provides per-sequence adjustment. Since Bk,c can absorb the average centering effect across training, the adaptive prior primarily regularizes per-sequence variation under label imbalance.

sections.content: Table 1 illustrates the asymmetric regularization under label imbalance using synthetic sequence with three genomic labels. The duration penalty νb,c grows linearly in k, so it primarily affects long segments of the dominant label, and are often the degenerate segmentations one wishes to suppress. Rare labels with low or negative baselines incur minimal penalty or slight boost, and their centered emissions at active positions have higher contrast (+8.12 for Promoter) than for dominant labels (+0.75 for Intron), because centering subtracts baseline close to the active-position emission for prevalent labels. Under balanced proportions (0.33 each), segment counts converge across centering modes. Architectural context. When the semi-CRF serves as an output decoder, the encoder can learn representations calibrated to the centering, and the adaptive prior provides robustness to emission scale miscalibration during early training. When semi-CRF marginals serve as input features for downstream model, path-invariant centering (bt = maxc fθ(t, c)) is preferable to preserve the canonical semi-CRF distribution.

sections.title: 5 Streaming Forward-Backward Algorithm
sections.content: We now present the streaming forward-backward algorithm that maintains only O(KC) working memory through ring buffers, with gradient checkpointing at intervals. The design follows the tiling and recomputation pattern of FlashAttention [13] and the chunked parallel scan of Mamba [14], adapted to the semi-Markov recurrence structure. 5 Duration penalty ν Imbalanced Balanced Label νb,c k=10 k=25 k=50 mean none mean none INTRON EXON UTR 2.021 -0.413 -0.603 -20.21 4.13 6.03 -50.53 10.32 15.07 -101.05 20.64 30. 90 64 59 142 35 19 80 80 76 84 80 77 Table 2: Implicit duration prior from mean centering under label imbalance. The per-label temporal mean νb,c creates duration penalty νb,c that penalizes long segments of prevalent labels. Right columns show decoded segment counts: under imbalance (75/15/10%), mean centering redistributes segments from the dominant to rare labels; under balanced proportions (0.33 each), the effect vanishes. Separately, cumulative score magnitudes at =50,000 confirm that mean centering grows as O( 1.28) versus O(T ) without centering (peak/ ) (peak/ 484).

sections.title:
5.1 Forward Scan with Ring Buffer
Algorithm 1 maintains a ring buffer α ∈ RK×C storing the K most recent log-forward messages
(Figure 1). At each position t, the recurrence looks back at most K positions, so a ring buffer indexed
by t mod K suffices–the oldest entry is always the one about to be overwritten.
sections.content: At checkpoint boundaries (every positions), the algorithm normalizes the ring buffer by subtracting the current maximum, accumulating the shift into Naccum. This prevents the log-forward messages from drifting to extreme magnitudes over long sequences [13]. The full ring buffer state Ωi and cumulative normalizer Ni are saved at each checkpoint for use in the backward pass. For variable-length batches, the normalization shift is masked to zero for positions beyond the true sequence length Lb, preventing phantom normalization of padding positions. sections.title: 5.2 Backward Scan and the 2K Ring Buffer
sections.content: The backward pass processes checkpoint segments in reverse order. For each segment [i, (i+1)), the algorithm restores α from the saved checkpoint Ωi, recomputes the local forward messages within the segment, then scans β right-to-left, computing marginals and accumulating gradients at each position (Figure 1b). The saved normalizer Ni restores the true scale of α when computing marginals via log µ = α + ψ + β + Ni log Z. The full backward algorithm is given in Appendix A. While the forward pass uses ring buffer of size (sufficient since we only look back positions), the backward pass requires ring buffer of size 2K. This asymmetry arises from write-before-read hazard in the backward access pattern: at position we read β values from positions {t+1, . . . , t+K} and write β[t], so with slots the write at collides with the read at t+K (both map to mod K), overwriting β[t+K] before it is read. Doubling the buffer to 2K slots separates write and read indices for the entire lookback window, eliminating the conflict. The additional KC float memory overhead is negligible compared to the O((cid:112)T /K KC) checkpoint storage. sections.title: 5.3 Complexity
sections.content: Table 3 summarizes the time and memory complexity of the streaming algorithm. The dominant working memory is the O(KC) ring buffer, which is independent of sequence length . Checkpoints contribute O((cid:112)T /K KC), which grows sublinearly in . The total time is O(T KC 2), the same as standard semiCRF forward-backward pass. The dominant memory reduction is replacing the O(T KC 2) edge tensor with the O(T C) prefix-sum array (a factor of KC). The ring buffer further reduces forward-message storage from O(T C) to O(KC), independent of sequence length. At = 106, = 200, = 6, the edge tensor would require 29 GB in float32; the prefix-sum array requires 24 MB, and the ring buffer 10 KB.

sections.title: 6 Experiments
sections.content: We evaluate the ability of Flash-SemiCRF to perform exact semi-Markov inference across range of values of , K, and C, scaling from NER-sized problems to genome-scale sequences, measuring time, memory, and throughput. We further show that the natural candidates for exploiting bounded banded or blocksparse matrix formats cannot prevent the near-dense intermediates that arise under composition in parallel-scan formulation. Next, we benchmark linear CRF (K=1) and semi-CRF (K=30) on the DARPA TIMIT speech corpus [16]. Before presenting scaling benchmarks, we address whether tree-structured alternatives could exploit the bounded via banded kernels.

sections.title: 6.1 Infeasibility of Banded Backends for Tree-Structured Inference
sections.content: Bounded segment length suggests that tree-structured backends could exploit sparsity via banded or block-sparse kernels. Two structural results show they cannot (full proofs in Appendix C). First, at tree node of span S, the feasible duration constraint d1 + d2 is anti-diagonal triangular, not diagonal-banded; the clique formed by all states with S/2 forces permutation-independent bandwidth lower bound of CS/2 1, so the compatibility matrix is near-dense once K. Second, even genuinely banded one-step operators fill in under composition: bw(Bm) = min(T, mK), saturating at dense width after O(log(T /K)) tree levels. Empirically, bandwidth-reducing permutations yield ratios bwbest/(n1) 0.97 for K, and block-triangular formats that match the triangular geometry did not yield speedups due to indirect indexing overhead. Consequently, banded storage cannot prevent the near-dense intermediates that drive the high memory requirements in tree-structured formulations, motivating the streaming approach of Section 5. Similarly, pytorch-struct implements heuristic of KC > 200 state space switch to use the linear scan backend instead of the default tree search approach.

sections.title: 6.2 Benchmarking
sections.content: Time and Memory We benchmark semi-CRF backends across , K, and  (Fig. 2)  . Tree-based methods (binary tree, memory sharded tree) achieve the lowest per-position latency at small state sizes but degrade rapidly as (K+1)C increases (Fig. 2A, B), reflecting the cost of materializing O(T (KC)2) intermediates. Linear and streaming scans exhibit stable, state-size-independent compute scaling; the fused Triton kernel matches this stability while improving throughput over unfused linear scans through on-the-fly edge evaluation (Fig. 2E). The dominant constraint at scale is memory, not compute. Linear and streaming scans maintain small, constant memory footprint regardless of , while tree-based and block-structured methods require orders of magnitude more memory (Fig. 2C). This separation is most visible in the out-of-memory (OOM) frontier (Fig. 2D, F): tree-based methods support progressively smaller state sizes as grows, whereas the fused kernel maintains nearly constant maximum state size. Eliminating edge tensor materialization fundamentally alters the scaling behavior of semi-CRF inference. To assess performance in regimes relevant to genomics-scale segmentation (large and K), we further evaluate the fused Triton implementation at extended sequence lengths and segment sizes (2G).In this setup, the tradeoff between memory and per-position runtime for K=20008000 is driven by hardwarespecific constraints, likely due to L1 cache saturation, and will vary across systems. Further, in this setup where the competing methods are no longer tractable due to memory constraints, the fused kernel maintains near-linear runtime scaling in and stable memory footprint, demonstrating practical applicability to kilobase-scale segmentation tasks. DARPA TIMIT Speech Corpus To evaluate Flash-SemiCRF as training and inference layer on real segmentation task, we benchmark on the DARPA TIMIT acoustic-phonetic corpus [16], standard testbed for structured sequence models in speech recognition. TIMIT is natural fit for semi-CRFs because phonemes have characteristic, label-dependent durations (vowels persist longer than stops, silence segments are highly variable) that duration-aware model can exploit. TIMIT provides 630 speakers (462 train, 168 test) with time-aligned phoneme annotations at 61 phone classes, collapsed to the standard 39-phone set (C = 39, CPAD = 64) following the Lee and Hon convention [17]. Input features are 13 MFCCs with delta and delta-delta coefficients (39 dimensions total) extracted at 10 ms frame shift, z-score normalized using training-set statistics. Fig. 2: Theoretical Benchmarks. A. Compute time per positions relationship to model state size between backends across different sizes T. B. Compute time for forward and backwards pass between each backend. C. Peak Memory utilization between backends at T=128, K=4, C=3. D. Mapping the out of memory (OOM) frontier for each backend across different sizes of T. E. Comparing fused Triton kernel optimization to original linear scan and binary tree backends (pytorch-struct) for compute time per position across different sizes F. Demonstrating Triton implementation maintains linear memory requirements across sizes while Binary tree is able to be used as viable compute backend. G. Demonstration of triton streaming backend performance at scales T=50,000, C=6, batch size=32 and variable size K. 11 Experimental setup. Both models share an identical 3-layer bidirectional LSTM encoder (hidden dimension 256, dropout 0.3) followed by the Flash-SemiCRF structured decoder, trained with AdamW (η = 103, weight decay 105) and cosine annealing over 50 epochs (batch size 32). CRF parameters receive L2 regularization (λ = 0.01) and gradient clipping at norm 5.0. The partition function is computed in float64 for numerical stability. Learnable scalar sequence boundary parameters πstart, πend RC are folded into the cumulative scores via the prefix-sum trick described in Appendix D.4. The only difference between models is the maximum segment duration: = 1 (linear CRF) versus = 30 (semi-CRF). This adds = 1,170 duration bias parameters to the semi-CRF, negligible relative to the encoders 1.6M parameters. At CPAD = 64, the per-checkpoint gradient workspace (Section A.4.4) requires 120 MB for batch of 32, well within the regime where workspace memory is eclipsed by the edge tensor savings. Convergence. Figure 3 shows training convergence across three metrics. Both models converge to similar final Phone Error Rates (PER = 0.218 for K=30 vs. 0.219 for K=1; best PER 0.2178 vs. 0.2187 at epoch 43), consistent with the expectation that strong neural encoder absorbs much of the segmentation structure into its learned representations, compressing the marginal contribution of explicit duration modeling to corpus-level PER. The semi-CRF maintains small but consistent advantage in boundary F1 (0.476 vs. 0.468) and segment F1 (0.215 vs. 0.207), reflecting improved segmentation coherence from explicit duration modeling. The semi-CRF starts behind (PER = 0.95 at epoch 0 vs. 0.89 for K=1), requiring additional warmup to explore the larger state space, but overtakes the linear CRF by epoch 5 on PER and by epoch 1 on boundary and segment F1. The PER gap peaks near epoch 20 ( 0.005) and compresses to 0.001 as both models converge, suggesting that harder tasks with larger or may exhibit more pronounced semi-CRF advantage. Validation loss is consistently higher for the semi-CRF (206.8 vs. 183.7 at epoch 50) despite its lower PER. This is expected: the semi-CRF partition function sums over combinatorially larger space of valid (K, C) segmentations, distributing probability mass more broadly than the per-frame linear CRF. The discrepancy underscores that the negative log-likelihood and the MAP segmentation accuracy measure distinct quantities. Boundary entropy Hbdy Position entropy Hpos Validation NLL 5.687 (invariant) 3.651 183.7 0.218 0.476 0.919 0.215 5.529 3.651 206.8 Table 6: TIMIT benchmark results (C = 39, 50 epochs, best checkpoint by PER at epoch 43). Both models share the same BiLSTM encoder (1.6M parameters); the semi-CRF adds = 1,170 duration bias parameters. Boundary entropy for the linear CRF is structurally fixed: p(boundary t) = 1 when K=1. Throughput. Table 7 compares wall-clock training and inference time on single L40S GPU. The pytorch-struct linear scan [6] requires 4,430 per training epoch and 1,243 per inference pass at K=30, making 50-epoch experiment multi-day commitment. Flash-SemiCRF reduces this to 175 per epoch (25 speedup) and 7 per inference pass (178), processing 6.5k frames/s versus 0.3k. Despite the 30 increase in theoretical O(T KC 2) complexity from K=1 to K=30, training the semi-CRF takes only 6.5 longer per epoch: 175 versus 27 s. Inference time for K=1 (32 s) is paradoxically slower than for K=30 (7 s). The K=1 CRF forward pass is fast (as reflected in the 27 training time, which is dominated by forward-backward); the bottleneck 12 Backend Model Train (s/ep) Infer (s) utt/s kfr/s pytorch-struct linear scan Flash-SemiCRF (Triton) Flash-SemiCRF (PyTorch) Semi-CRF Semi-CRF Linear CRF 30 30 1 Speedup (Flash K=30 vs. pytorch-struct) 4,430 175 27 25 1,243 7 32 178 0.8 21.1 140 26 0.3 6.5 42.8 22 Table 7: Wall-clock training and inference time on TIMIT (single NVIDIA L40S GPU, = 32, = 39). Flash-SemiCRF achieves 25 training speedup and 178 inference speedup over pytorch-structs linear scan, the only existing backend that can run K=30 at this scale without OOM (tree backends exceed memory at (K+1)C = 1,209; see Fig. 2D). All three backends compute the same partition function (Table 5d). is the unfused PyTorch Viterbi decode, which runs outside the Triton kernel pipeline. The K3 path dispatches to the fused Triton kernel for both forward-backward and Viterbi (Appendix D.9), avoiding this overhead. fused K=1 Triton Viterbi was not prioritized since the training loop, where kernel fusion provides the dominant speedup, already dispatches efficiently at K=1. Boundary entropy and structural differences in uncertainty. The clearest separation between models appears in boundary entropy (Figure 3). Complementing the perposition label entropy Hpos (the sequence-averaged form of the frame-level class entropy used by Salvi [18] for phonetic boundary detection), we define sequence-level diagnostic as the Shannon entropy [19] of the normalized boundary distribution: Hbdy = (cid:88) t=1 ˆpt ln ˆpt, ˆpt = (cid:80) p(boundary t) p(boundary t) , (6) where p(boundary t) is the posterior segment-start probability from the forward-backward algorithm, and exp(Hbdy) gives the effective number of boundary positions. For K=1, every frame is its own segment, so ˆpt = 1/T and Hbdy = ln per utterance regardless of learned parameters; the linear CRF does not represent boundary uncertainty. Averaged over the test corpus, the K=1 invariant is E[ln ] = 5.687 (geometric mean length 295 frames). The semi-CRF reduces Hbdy from 5.671 at initialization to 5.529 by epoch 50, concentrating probability mass at true phoneme transitions; the effective boundary count exp(Hbdy) drops from 295 to 252. Per-position label entropy converges to the same value for both models (Hpos = 3.651), indicating that the encoder representations are comparably expressive and the difference lies in the structural decoder. Per-utterance uncertainty analysis. The corpus-level PER gap ( = 0.001) averages over utterances where the models perform comparably and utterances where they diverge. Figure 4 shows posterior marginals p(c t) and boundary posteriors for DR4 MPCS0 SI729 (T = 480, 39 ground-truth segments), where the semi-CRF achieves PER = 0.232 versus the linear CRFs 0.411, 44% relative reduction concentrated in regions of phonetic ambiguity. Both models activate similar label hypotheses, but the semi-CRF concentrates posterior mass more tightly along the correct label rows. The boundary posterior panel makes the structural difference explicit: the linear CRF assigns p(boundary t) = 1.0 uniformly (expected at K=1), while the semi-CRFs boundary posterior rises at true phoneme boundaries (red dashed lines) and falls to < 0.2 in segment interiors. The semi-CRF thus provides posterior over segmentation structure that per-position model cannot represent, useful for downstream tasks requiring boundary uncertainty. 13 Fig. 3: Training convergence on TIMIT: K=1 linear CRF vs. K=30 semi-CRF. Both models share an identical BiLSTM encoder; the only difference is the maximum segment duration. The semi-CRF maintains similar Phone Error Rate (PER) as the linear CRF, but consistent improvement at boundary and segment F1 despite starting from higher initial PER (larger state space requires additional warmup). Fig. 4: Per-utterance posterior analysis on TIMIT utterance DR4 MPCS0 SI729 (T = 480 frames). Top: Ground-truth (GT) phoneme segmentation. Middle panels: Position-marginal heatmaps p(c t) for the semi-CRF (K=30, PER = 0.232) and linear CRF (K=1, PER = 0.411). Both models activate similar label hypotheses, but the semi-CRF concentrates posterior mass more tightly on correct labels. Bottom: Boundary posterior p(boundary t). The semi-CRF (blue) varies across the utterance, peaking at true phoneme boundaries (red dashed lines) and suppressing in segment interiors. The linear CRF (orange) assigns p(boundary t) = 1.0 uniformly, the expected behavior when every frame is its own segment (K=1).

sections.title: 7 Discussion
sections.content: The central barrier to scaling semi-Markov Conditional Random Fields has not been their asymptotic complexity, but the memory constraints of the standard implementations. Existing approaches materialize an O(T KC 2) edge tensor, making inference memory bound and infeasible on long sequences. By eliminating this tensor through prefix-sum decomposition and streaming dynamic programming, FlashSemiCRF reframes semi-CRF inference as compute-bound problem. This shift mirrors recent advances such as FlashAttention and scan-based sequence models, where avoiding intermediate materialization, not just changing asymptotics, enables orders of magnitude improvements in scale. This change in computational approach has direct modeling consequences. Semi-CRFs, long limited to short sequences in domains such as natural language processing, now become viable as structured decoders for long-range sequence modeling. This addresses persistent limitation of modern neural sequence models: while they learn rich contextual representations, they typically operate under perposition prediction paradigm that does not enforce globally consistent segmentations. In contrast, semiCRFs ensure coherent partitions of the input and model the label-dependent duration explicitly. With Flash-SemiCRF, these properties can now be applied at scale, enabling segmentation tasks to combine learned representations with explicit structural constraints (such as event detection, temporal action segmentation, or domain parsing). Both linear and semi-CRF models have complementary strengths that make them useful in different parts of genomics workflow. Linear CRFs are computationally efficient and well-suited for annotating known sequence structures (e.g., Tranquillyzer, Bonito/Dorado). In contrast, semi-CRFs provide modeling of segment structure and boundary uncertainty, which can be advantageous for more complex annotation tasks, albeit at the cost of increased computational overhead due to the larger context space and exact marginals. The edge potential decomposition (3.4) also has consequences for gradient flow and encoder design. Because the boundary projections start, end and the content emissions fθ enter as separate additive terms, their gradients are structurally decoupled: the Jacobian is block-diagonal in (W start, end) vs. fθ, so parameter updates can adjust boundary and content scoring independently. In joint edge tensor parameterization these error signals are entangled, which can slow convergence when boundary and interior features have different characteristic scales or noise profiles. The additive decomposition also provides natural interface for multi-modal encoders. Modalities that produce punctate, position-specific signals (e.g., Tn5 insertion densities from ATAC-seq) can be routed to the boundary projections via modality-specific heads on ht, while broad-domain signals (e.g., aggregate expression counts, histone mark coverage) feed the content term through fθ and accumulate evidence via the prefix sum. This routing is possible precisely because the terms are precomputed independently; it would not be available in parameterization where boundary and content features are jointly materialized. Beyond computational efficiency, the formulation introduces subtle but important statistical effect. The sequence-level centering of emissions induces an adaptive, data-dependent duration prior Beff k,c = Bk,c νb,c k. This acts as an implicit regularizer: labels with high average emissions incur stronger penalties for long segments, while low-prevalence labels receive relative boost. In imbalanced settings, this mechanism suppresses degenerate segmentations dominated by frequent states and improves recovery of rare classes. At the same time, because this transformation is not path-invariant, it alters the underlying distribution and may be undesirable in settings requiring strict probabilistic interpretation. The choice between centered and uncentered formulations therefore reflects trade-off between numerical stability, regularization, and model fidelity. We note limitation regarding the state space size C. To ensure deterministic gradients and eliminate inter-segment atomic contention on the GPU, Flash-SemiCRF allocates gradient workspace of size O(B Nckpt 2). While this introduces quadratic dependence on the number of labels, genomic segmentation tasks, parsing promoters, UTRs, exons, introns, and intergenic regions, rarely require > 64. Within this regime, the workspace memory remains low and is entirely eclipsed by the savings from avoiding the O(T KC 2) edge tensor materialization. The implementation trades asymptotic scaling in for transformative scaling in the dimensions that define genomic workloads (T and K). more consequential constraint in practice is the maximum segment duration K. Segments longer than cannot receive unified duration or content score and are instead captured through K-position lookback and boundary projections alone, sacrificing the full segment-level modeling that motivates the semi-CRF. In human genomics, median protein-coding exons (130 bp) and most introns (1,539 bp median) fall well within practical values, but the 95th percentile intron length (22,908 bp) approaches the regime where runtime and register pressure costs become significant (Section 5.4). Selecting to cover 15 the segments of greatest modeling interest is therefore the primary tuning decision, and characterizing how segmentation quality degrades as features extend past is an important direction for future work. From systems perspective, the results highlight broader principle: structured models are often dismissed as unscalable due to nominal complexity, when in practice the limiting factor is memory traffic and tensor materialization. By converting semi-CRF inference into fused, streaming kernel, we demonstrate that exact structured inference can achieve high throughput and full hardware utilization at sequence lengths exceeding 106. This suggests that other structured models based on dynamic programming over variable-length segments, such as segmental RNNs [4], may admit similar reformulations. Acknowledgements. We gratefully acknowledge the feedback on this work from Brad Dickson. Computation for the work described in this paper was supported by the High Performance Cluster and Cloud Computing (HPC3) Resource at the Van Andel Research Institute.

B.4 Backend 1: Binary Tree (Parallel Scan) The binary tree algorithm is the default semi-Markov implementation in pytorch-struct [6]. It reformulates the forward recursion as semiring matrix products over an expanded state space of size = (K + 1) C, where each state (k, c) represents time steps remaining in segment of label (see Rush [6] for the full transition matrix construction). In PyTorch/CUDA experiments, block-triangular storage did not yield speedups at typical sparsity levels. The overhead of indirect indexing and non-contiguous memory access outweighed savings from skipping 50% of blocks. GPUs prefer well-tuned dense kernels unless sparsity exceeds 90%. B.6 Backend 3: Linear Scan The standard linear scan is the reference semi-Markov implementation in pytorch-struct [6]. It implements the textbook semi-CRF forward recursion αt(c) = LSEk,c[ αtk(c)+ ψ(t, k, c, c)] sequentially over positions, avoiding the O((KC)2) intermediate storage of tree backends. vectorized variant replaces inner loops with batched tensor operations, yielding 23 speedup. Complexity. Time: O(T KC 2). Space: O(T C) for forward messages, or O(KC) with ring buffer; in both cases the pre-materialized edge tensor adds O(T KC 2), which dominates. Depth: O(T ). B.7 Backend 4: Streaming Linear Scan The streaming linear scan reduces forward message memory from O(T C) to O(KC) via ring buffer ( αt(c) = α[t mod K, c]). However, this PyTorch implementation still requires the pre-materialized edge tensor as input. Complexity. Time: O(T KC 2). Space: O(T KC 2) for edge tensor + O(KC) for ring buffer. True -independent memory requires computing edges on-the-fly via the prefix-sum decomposition (main text, Eq. (4)), achieved only in the fused Triton kernel (Appendix A). B.8 Complexity Summary B.9 Duration Distributions The duration bias Bk,c can be learned as free parameters or constrained to standard parametric forms (Geometric, Negative Binomial, Poisson, Uniform), reducing the parameter count to O(C) and encoding inductive bias about expected segment lengths. 21 Backend Work Memory Depth Notes O(T n3) Binary tree (dense) O(T n3) Binary tree (sharded) O(T n3) Block-triangular O(T n3) Banded O(T KC 2) Linear scan Streaming linear (PyTorch) O(T KC 2) O(T n2) n2) O( 1 2 O(T n2) O(T n2) O(T KC 2) O(T KC 2) < 100 < 150 O(log ) O( ) O(log ) Not faster O(log ) Not viable O(T ) O(T ) General Ring buffer for α O(T KC 2) O((cid:112)T /K KC) Streaming (Triton kernel) Genome-scale Table B.2: Backend comparison. = (K + 1) C. O(T ) 22 Appendix Infeasibility of Banded Backends for Tree-Structured Semi-CRF Inference This appendix analyzes natural optimization for exact semi-CRF [1] inference: using banded or blocksparse matrix kernels inside binary-tree (parallel-scan) formulation of the dynamic program. The conclusion is negative: within this formulation, banded storage can reduce cost only at the smallest spans, but cannot prevent near-dense intermediate operators at the levels that dominate runtime and memory. Scope. The claims below apply to exact tree-structured implementations that represent node-to-node combination as semiring matrix products over an expanded state space of size Θ(KC). This does not rule out approximate inference (e.g., pruning) or alternative exact formulations that do not materialize these operators. C.1 Notation We consider sequence of length , equivalently boundary indices 0, 1, . . . , . The maximum segment length is K, and the label/state count is C. Two matrices are used as simplifying models: Boundary reachability (global boolean model). Define {0, 1}(T +1)(T +1) by B[i, j] = (cid:40) 1 0 if 1 otherwise. (C.1) This captures the monotone-time constraint single segment advances forward by at most boundaries. For instance, with = 5 and = 2, the matrix has B[0, 1] = B[0, 2] = B[1, 2] = B[1, 3] = = 1, but B[0, 3] = 0 (since 3 0 = 3 > K). Duration compatibility at tree node (local pattern). At tree node of span length S, partial states are indexed by duration {1, . . . , D} where = min(K, S), and label {1, . . . , C}. The quantity caps the duration states at the actual feasible range for the current span: durations cannot exceed the span S, nor can they exceed the global maximum K. Feasible left/right duration pairs satisfy d1 + d2 S. For each feasible duration pair, label interactions are typically dense, yielding block-dense sparsity. Effective bandwidth. For square matrix whose indices have been flattened to {1, . . . , n}, define the (structural) bandwidth bw(M ) = max{ j : [i, j] is structurally nonzero }. (C.2) For boolean matrices, nonzero means 1; for semiring score matrices, it means not masked / not . banded representation with half-bandwidth requires bw(M ) (after any chosen permutation). C.2 Local Incompatibility Pattern Is Triangular, Not Diagonal-Banded At an internal tree node of span S, standard combine operation must account for left and right partial durations d1, d2 that together fit inside the span: d1 + d2 S. (C.3) In the (d1, d2) plane, this feasible set is an anti-diagonal triangle. diagonal band, by contrast, corresponds to d1 d2 b. These shapes overlap only partially, and the mismatch becomes pronounced as soon as is nontrivial fraction of K. To make not narrow-banded under any ordering precise, it suffices to lower bound the minimum achievable bandwidth. Proposition 1 (Bandwidth lower bound from clique) Fix S, let = min(K, S), and define binary matrix MS {0, 1}(DC)(DC) indexed by pairs (d, y) {1, . . . , D} {1, . . . , C}, with MS[(d1, y1), (d2, y2)] = 1 d1 + d2 S. (C.4) 23 For any simultaneous row/column permutation π, (cid:16) bw π MSPπ (cid:17) (cid:107) (cid:106) 2 1. Proof Let = S/2. For any d, m, + 2m S, so all states in = {1, . . . , m} {1, . . . , C} (C.5) (C.6) are mutually adjacent in the undirected graph induced by MS; hence is clique of size = mC. For any linear ordering of clique on vertices, the first and last vertices are adjacent, so the bandwidth is at least 1. Consequence. When is moderate (e.g., K) and is not tiny, the best achievable bandwidth is already large fraction of the dense width DC. In the extreme case 2Ksince = when K, the condition d1 + d2 with d1, d2 is always satisfied when 2Kthe matrix MS is structurally dense. C.3 Fill-In Under Composition in Boolean Reachability Model Even if the local operator were genuinely banded, repeated composition widens the support. Lemma 2 (Boolean powers widen linearly) Let be the boundary adjacency matrix defined above, and interpret multiplication over the boolean semiring (OR as addition, AND as multiplication). Then (Bm)[i, j] = 1 if and only if there exists path of exactly segments from to j. For 1, (Bm)[i, j] = 1 mK, (C.7) and therefore bw(Bm) = min(T, mK). Proof path of segments advances by δ1 + + δm with each δt {1, . . . , K}, hence mK. Conversely, if = satisfies mK, then can be written as sum of integers in [1, K] (e.g., start from all ones and distribute the remaining units without exceeding K). Implication for balanced binary tree. In balanced binary tree [22], combining two children corresponds (in this simplified reachability model) to composing reachability over larger number of segments. The relevant parameter therefore grows geometrically with tree level, so bw(Bm) increases rapidly and saturates at the dense width on the order of log2(T /K) levels. The reachability pattern becomes near-dense well before the root, so banded container offers little benefit at the levels that dominate the total work. C.4 Why Matrix Reordering Helps Only at Very Small Spans reasonable countermeasure is to apply bandwidth-reducing permutations (e.g., Reverse Cuthill McKee [23]; see also [24]). Proposition 1 already limits what is possible at single node: cliques force large bandwidth regardless of ordering. At the global scale, once composition produces near-complete reachability, permutations cannot help substantially. One clean sufficient condition is undirected completeness: if for every unordered pair of indices {u, v} at least one of [u, v] or [v, u] is structurally nonzero, then the induced undirected graph is clique and the bandwidth is 1 under any ordering. Empirical bandwidth ratios. The accompanying bandwidth report (available on github with the codebase) evaluates several orderings across (K, C, S). Summarizing bwbest/(n 1) by span fraction S/K: for small spans (S K/2), bestcase bandwidth ratios ranged from 0.23 to 0.68 (mean 0.44); for moderate spans (K/2 < K), ratios ranged from 0.78 to 0.99 (mean 0.90); for large spans (S K), ratios were 0.97 and often exactly 1.0. Thus, reordering can reduce bandwidth when spans are very small, but offers negligible benefit once spans approach K, the regime that dominates higher tree levels. C.5 Practical Implications for Exact Semi-Markov Backends The computation and memory of tree-structured backends are dominated by higher tree levels, where local duration compatibility is near-dense (Proposition 1) and multi-step composition produces neardense support (Lemma 2). Block-triangular formats that match the triangular geometry reduce storage by roughly half, but in our experiments, indirect indexing overhead negated this at typical sparsity levels, consistent with the well-known GPU performance pattern that moderate block sparsity underperforms dense kernels unless sparsity exceeds 90%. If sparsity is essential, it must be introduced by approximation (e.g., segment filtering [12] or hybrid architectures [4, 5]), which enforce value sparsity rather than relying on structural sparsity that does not persist under composition. Appendix Linear and Near-Linear CRF Implementations This supplement documents the specialized = 1 (linear CRF) and = 2 (near-linear CRF) implementations in flash-semicrf. These optimized paths eliminate ring buffer overhead for common sequence labeling tasks while maintaining compatibility with the streaming Semi-CRF interface. D.1 Overview The streaming Semi-CRF module automatically dispatches to specialized implementations based on the maximum segment duration K: Implementation Rationale = 1 = 2 3 LinearCRFStreaming SemiCRFK2Streaming SemiCRFStreamingTriton Standard linear-chain CRF; no duration loop Explicit 2-step history; avoids ring buffer edge cases Full ring buffer architecture Table D.1: Automatic dispatch by maximum segment duration. When full boundary projections (P start, end) are active, = 1 and = 2 dispatch falls through to the generic 3 streaming path. Scalar boundaries (πstart, πend) are folded into and do not affect dispatch. Triton Kernel Scope. The specialized = 1 and = 2 paths are implemented only in PyTorch. The Triton streaming kernel requires 3 for correct ring buffer operation. This is intentional: the PyTorch implementations are already efficient for small K, and the Triton kernels complexity is justified only when ring buffers and checkpointing provide substantial memory savings. Tilde () denotes log-domain quantities. Durations are 1-indexed in the equations: {1, . . . , K}, stored at 0-indexed positions k1 in the implementation. D.3 Score Centering Score centering follows the main text (Section 3.2): the per-sequence baseline νb,c is subtracted from raw projected scores before constructing the cumulative sum S, controlling cumulative magnitude from O(T ) to O( ). For variable-length batches, the baseline is computed as masked mean over the Lb valid positions (see Appendix for padding semantics). Invariance of emissions. Centering does not change the per-position emission scores: St,c St1,c = fθ(t1, c) νb,c, and the constant νb,c cancels in the forward recurrence (it shifts all labels equally at each timestep). However, centering keeps the absolute magnitude of bounded, which matters for content scores spanning many positions when > 1. D.4 Sequence Boundary Handling flash-semicrf supports two boundary mechanisms that control how sequence start and end positions are scored. These interact differently with the = 1 and = 2 fast paths. D.4.1 Scalar Boundaries (Zero-Overhead Folding) When use sequence boundaries=True, learnable vectors πstart RC and πend RC are activated (initialized to zero, equivalent to pytorch-crfs start transitions/end transitions). These add label-dependent scores at sequence boundaries: πstart rewards (or penalizes) label at the start of sequence, and πend at the end. Rather than threading boundary parameters through the forward and backward kernels, scalar boundaries are folded directly into the cumulative scores via prefix-sum arithmetic: Sb,0,c Sb,0,c πstart Sb,Lb,c Sb,Lb,c + πend (D.1) (D.2) Why this works. The content score for segment [s, t) is St,c Ss,c. By subtracting πstart from position 0: Any segment starting at = 0 gets content score (St,c S0,c) + πstart Segments not starting at 0 are unaffected (their Ss,c and St,c both remain unchanged). Similarly, adding πend to position Lb adds it to any segment ending at = Lb. . Consequence for dispatch. Because the folding modifies before the forward/backward functions are called, the = 1 and = 2 fast paths remain active. The LinearCRFStreaming and SemiCRFK2Streaming autograd functions are boundary-agnosticthey operate on the already-modified with zero additional overhead. Gradient flow. Gradients with respect to πstart and πend flow automatically through the modified cumulative scores via standard autograd: πstart πend L = Sb,0,cL = Sb,Lb,cL (D.3) (D.4) D.4.2 Full Boundary Projections (Fallthrough to Generic Path) When use boundary projections=True, position-dependent boundary scores start t,c are computed by linear layers applied to the encoder hidden states. These provide richer expressiveness than scalar boundaries, allowing the model to learn position-specific start/end preferences. and end t,c 26 When full boundary projections are active, the = 1 and = 2 fast paths are skipped. Dispatch falls through to the generic 3 streaming path, which handles boundaries via the HAS BOUNDARIES flag in the Triton kernel (or the PyTorch reference equivalent). Composition with scalar boundaries. If both use sequence boundaries and use boundary projections are enabled, the scalar vectors are added to the projection tensors at the boundary positions: b,0,c start start b,Lb1,c end end b,0,c + πstart b,Lb1,c + πend (D.5) (D.6) In this case the boundary projections trigger the generic path regardless, so the scalars compose additively within that path rather than folding into S. Rationale for fallthrough. The full boundary projections require per-position boundary scores to be threaded through the forward recurrence. The generic kernel already supports this via HAS BOUNDARIES. Duplicating that logic in the specialized = 1/K = 2 PyTorch paths would add complexity for niche use case. D.5 K=1: Linear CRF When = 1, every segment has duration 1, reducing the Semi-CRF to standard linear-chain CRF. The forward recurrence simplifies to: αt(c) = emitt(c) + log (cid:88) c= exp(cid:0) αt1(c) + Tc,c (cid:1) where the emission score is computed via prefix-sum difference: emitt(c) = St,c St1,c + B1,c (D.7) (D.8) When scalar boundaries are active, the boundary scores are already embedded in (Section D.4.1), at = Lb without any modification at = 1 and πend so the recurrence above implicitly includes πstart to the algorithm. At = 1: α1(c) = emit1(c) + log (cid:88) c= exp(Tc,c) (D.9) (D.10) Interaction with Sequence Boundaries. When use sequence boundaries=True, learnable parameters πstart are activated (initialized to zero). These are not applied by modifying α0instead they are folded into via the prefix-sum trick (Section D.4.1). The forward algorithm still sees α0(c) = 0; the boundary effect appears in the emission scores. At initialization (πstart = 0), behavior is identical to the boundary-free case. After training, πstart and πend provide independent expressiveness beyond the transition matrix. and πend c D.5.2 Algorithm Algorithm D.1 presents the optimized = 1 forward pass. 27 Algorithm D.1: Linear CRF Forward (K = 1) Input: Cumulative scores RB(T +1)C, Transitions RCC, Duration bias R1C (optional), Lengths ℓ ZB Output: Log partition log RB 1 α 0 RBC ; 2 for 1 to do 3 et S[:, t, :] S[:, 1, :] + B1 ; // Standard linear CRF recurrence αnew LogSumExpc( α[:, c] + Tc,:) + et; // Update only active sequences α where(t ℓ, αnew, α); 5 6 log LogSumExpc( α[ℓ]) ; 7 return log Z; // Uniform initialization // Emission // Final reduction Note. When scalar boundaries are active, has already been modified per Equations (D.1)(D.2). The algorithm is unchanged; the boundary scores appear implicitly in the emission differences. D.5.3 Complexity Time: O(T 2) matrix-vector products at each timestep Space: O(BC) single α vector per batch element No checkpointing: Full α history stored for backward pass (O(BT C)) The space overhead for storing full α history is acceptable because = 1 implies short-to-moderate sequences where the O(BT C) cost is manageable. D.6 K=2: Near-Linear CRF When = 2, segments can have duration 1 or 2. Rather than using the general ring buffer (which has edge cases at = 2 due to modular arithmetic with small indices), we use explicit 2-step history variables. Because these paths do not use checkpointing, the full α history is stored during the forward pass. Boundary transparency. When scalar boundaries are active, the backward pass requires no special handling because πstart and πend have already been folded into (Section D.4.1). Gradients with respect to πstart flow automatically through the modified cumulative scores via standard autograd. and πend D.7.1 Marginal Computation The marginal probability for segment spanning [t k, 1] with transition is: µ(t, k, c, c) = exp(cid:0) αtk(c) + ψ(t, k, c, c) + βt(c)(cid:1) exp(log Z) where the edge potential is: ψ(t, k, c, c) = (St,c Stk,c) + Bk,c + Tc,c (D.16) (D.17) Numerical safeguard. In the PyTorch reference implementation, the log marginal αtk(c) + ψ(t, k, c, c) + βt(c) log is clamped to [80, 80] before exponentiation. This prevents overflow/underflow when individual terms are 29 large in absolute value but nearly cancel. The clamped range corresponds to exp(80) 5.5 1034, well within float64 range. D.7.2 Gradient Formulas Cumulative Scores. The gradient for St,c accumulates contributions from segments ending at (positive) and starting after (negative): St,c = (cid:88) µ(t, k, c, c) µ(t + k, k, c, c) (D.18) (cid:88) k,c k,c Transitions. The gradient sums marginals over all positions and durations: Tc,c = (cid:88) L Zb (cid:88) t,k µb(t, k, c, c) Duration Bias. The gradient for duration sums over all segments of that duration: Bk,c = (cid:88) Zb (cid:88) t,c µb(t, k, c, c) (D.19) (D.20) D.8 Viterbi Decoding Both = 1 and = 2 implementations support Viterbi decoding for MAP inference. The algorithms follow the same structure as the forward pass, replacing LogSumExp with max and maintaining backpointers. D.8.1 K=1 Viterbi Standard linear CRF Viterbi with backpointers for the best previous label at each position. D.8.2 K=2 Viterbi Extended backpointers track both the best previous label and the best duration (1 or 2) at each position. The traceback reconstructs the segmentation by stepping back by the recorded duration. D.9 Why Triton Kernels Require The Triton streaming kernel is designed for the general Semi-CRF case where ring buffers and checkpointing provide substantial memory savings. For small K, ring buffer indexing creates edge cases: = 1: All positions map to ring index 0. The ring buffer degenerates to single slot, providing no benefit over simple variable. = 2: Indices alternate between 0 and 1. Wraparound logic is fragile and provides minimal memory savings over explicit variables. 3: Ring buffer provides meaningful history separation. Checkpointing amortizes over multiple segments, justifying the implementation complexity. The PyTorch implementations for = 1 and = 2 are already efficient (no kernel launch overhead, simple control flow), making Triton acceleration unnecessary. D.

sections.title: Implementation Summary
sections.content: Automatic Dispatch. The semi crf streaming forward function automatically routes to the appropriate implementation based on K. Users need not manage dispatch manually. 30 Time Space Checkpointing Ring Buffer Backend O(T 2) O(T 2) O(BC) 1 2 O(BC) 3 O(T KC 2) O(BKC) No No Yes No No Yes PyTorch PyTorch Triton (GPU) / PyTorch (CPU) Table D.3: Implementation characteristics by maximum segment duration. D.11 Functional Equivalence All three implementations (K = 1, = 2, 3) compute the same partition function and gradients for their respective segment duration constraints. Specifically: The = 1 path produces identical results to the general streaming kernel with = 1 (verified numerically). The = 2 path produces identical results to the general streaming kernel with = 2 (verified numerically). All paths support variable-length batches with proper masking. The equivalence between = 1/K = 2 fast paths and the generic kernel holds only when full boundary projections are not active. When start or end are provided, dispatch bypasses the specialized paths entirely (Section D.4.2), so the question of equivalence does not arise. Scalar boundaries (πstart, πend) are folded into before dispatch and do not affect equivalence. The specialized paths exist purely for performance optimization, not behavioral differences.
